ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for char siew / Chinese BBQ pork. FOODON_03302051 (pork food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pig meat products) is the closest parent. No specific char siew entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226912009 (Pork) is the closest available concept. No specific char siew concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.description: 1 small portion (approx. 150 g), suitable for 1–2 persons as part of a shared meal
serving.natural_language_portion: A small serving of char siew typically weighs approximately 150 g, comprising several slices of glazed roasted pork, and is commonly shared between one to two diners as part of a rice or noodle meal.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew (Barbecued Pork) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists char siew (barbecued pork) as a distinct entry. Values per 100 g are taken directly from that record. The input record describes a small portion (1–2 pax) sold at SGD 8.00, consistent with a standard Singapore hawker/restaurant char siew portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based dishes with sweet glaze — analogous estimate based on pork with honey-soy marinade
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for char siew exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the analogous profile of a sweet-glazed roasted pork dish: the dominant macronutrient is protein and fat, with moderate sugar from the honey-hoisin glaze. The carbohydrate content (~12.6 g/100 g) is primarily from added sugars with a moderate glycemic response dampened by the high protein and fat matrix. GI 40 is consistent with published estimates for similar sweet-marinated pork preparations. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 12.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~680 mg/100 g; ~1020 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit char siew intake or request reduced-sauce portions, as a single 150 g serving contributes approximately 1020 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate with high added sugar from honey-hoisin glaze (~10.2 g sugar/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume char siew in modest portions, as the sweet glaze contributes approximately 15 g of added sugar per 150 g serving; pairing with low-GI vegetables and limiting rice quantity can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.1 g/100 g) and sodium (~680 mg/100 g) combined with cholesterol (~68 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit char siew to occasional consumption, as the combination of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and high sodium in a 150 g serving may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char siew (叉烧, also romanized as char siu or cha siu) is a classic Cantonese-style Chinese barbecued pork dish that is deeply embedded in Singaporean food culture. The dish is prepared by marinating strips or slabs of pork — typically pork shoulder, pork neck, or pork belly — in a sweet-savory mixture of soy sauce, hoisin sauce, honey, oyster sauce, five-spice powder, sugar, and sometimes red fermented tofu, which imparts a characteristic reddish hue. The marinated pork is then roasted or barbecued at high heat, producing a caramelized, lacquered exterior with a tender, juicy interior. The flavour profile is distinctly sweet, savory, and slightly smoky, with a sticky glaze that is a hallmark of the dish. In Singapore, char siew is commonly served sliced over steamed white rice (char siew rice), atop wonton noodles, or as a standalone roasted meat dish from Chinese roast meat stalls (siu mei stalls). Nutritionally, char siew is a moderate-energy food (~231 kcal/100 g) with a good protein content (~19.8 g/100 g) and moderate fat (~11.2 g/100 g). The carbohydrate content (~12.6 g/100 g) is largely from added sugars in the glaze, giving it a low glycemic index (~40) due to the protein-fat matrix. Sodium is notably high (~680 mg/100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for halal diets as it contains pork.

confidence_reason: Char siew is listed as a distinct food entry in the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Nutrient values are taken directly from that record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented.
notes: Serving size of 150 g is used as a representative small portion (1–2 pax) consistent with the input description. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for char siew. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.1 g/100 g, a trace amount consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in pork. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0 as char siew contains no meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 12.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.6. The input record notes this dish is served by Hennessy Duck - Funan on field survey data; the restaurant-level data is preserved in provenance but nutrient values are sourced from HPB Foodo, not the delivery platform.
